title: Merchandise exports by continent of destination
subtitle: Figures correspond to the value of [merchandise exports](#dod:merchandise-exports) by continental destination as a share of [gross domestic product](#dod:gdp) (GDP). All partner countries are classified into continent groupings according to OWID's classification.
caption:
Merchandise exports by continent of destination, India, 1949 to 2014
Figures correspond to the value of merchandise exports by continental destination as a share of gross domestic product (GDP). All partner countries are classified into continent groupings according to OWID's classification.

The stacked area chart tracks India's merchandise exports as a percentage of GDP from 1949 to 2014, broken down by the continent of destination. The vertical axis measures the export share from 0% to 18%, while the horizontal axis spans the 65-year period. Exports to Asia, represented by the blue area at the base, constitute the largest component for most of the timeline, starting near 2% in 1949 and rising to approximately 8% by 2014. Exports to Western Europe, shown in maroon, form the second largest layer, expanding significantly from under 1% in the 1960s to roughly 4% in 2014. The green band representing North America and the orange band for Africa show moderate growth, each occupying a smaller share of the total stack. Exports to Eastern Europe and Oceania appear as thin layers at the top, contributing minimal percentages throughout the period. The total height of the stack shows a general upward trend, beginning around 4% in 1949, dipping slightly in the 1960s, and climbing steadily after 1990 to reach a peak of over 16% around 2012 before settling near 16.5% in 2014. A sharp, narrow spike occurs around 2008, where the total export share briefly exceeds 18%. The data comes from dyadic transactions, and the series for Western Europe corresponds to the sum of exports between all Western European countries divided by the GDP of Western Europe. The visualization shows a structural shift toward higher export intensity relative to the size of the domestic economy over the latter half of the 20th century and into the 21st.
